mechanism_narrative: Sustained growth in retail sales (RSAFS) indicates robust consumer demand, which translates to higher corporate earnings expectations and drives S&P 500 forward returns over a 20-day horizon. This mechanism is considered always-on, reflecting the continuous impact of consumer health on the market.
failure_condition: If the 20-day forward returns for S&P 500 show no statistically significant positive correlation with RSAFS month-over-month growth over a 5-year rolling window, or the correlation turns negative.

mechanism_narrative: A significant drop in the unemployment rate (UNRATE) below a critical threshold signals a tightening labor market. This leads to increased wage growth and consumer spending, boosting corporate profits and equity valuations, resulting in positive S&P 500 forward returns over a 60-day horizon.
failure_condition: If, when UNRATE is below 4.5%, the S&P 500 60-day forward returns are negative or flat in more than 50% of triggered instances over a 10-year period.

mechanism_narrative: When inflation (CPIAUCSL) is high and the Federal Funds Rate (FEDFUNDS) is also high, it indicates an aggressive monetary tightening stance to combat inflation. This typically dampens economic activity and corporate earnings, leading to negative S&P 500 forward returns over the next 20 trading days.
failure_condition: If, during periods where both CPIAUCSL > 300 and FEDFUNDS > 4.0%, the S&P 500 20-day forward returns are predominantly positive (> 60% of observations) over a 15-year backtest.

mechanism_narrative: In a period of economic expansion, increasing industrial production (INDPRO) signals robust manufacturing activity and corporate profitability. This leads to short-term positive sentiment and S&P 500 returns over a 5-day horizon, as investors react quickly to signs of strong output.
failure_condition: If, during confirmed periods of economic expansion, a positive INDPRO growth rate does not correlate with positive S&P 500 5-day returns, or the correlation turns negative over a 10-year period.

mechanism_narrative: A sustained increase in housing starts (HOUST) indicates strong consumer and business confidence, which is a leading indicator for broader economic growth. This positive sentiment translates into positive S&P 500 performance over a longer 60-day horizon as economic activity picks up.
failure_condition: If, following a 3-month average growth in HOUST exceeding 5%, the S&P 500 60-day forward returns are negative in more than 40% of occurrences over a 15-year backtest.

mechanism_narrative: Strong non-farm payroll employment (PAYEMS) growth indicates a healthy labor market and robust economic activity. This supports consumer spending and corporate earnings, leading to positive S&P 500 returns over a 20-day horizon as market participants price in improved economic outlooks.
failure_condition: If the S&P 500 20-day forward returns do not show a statistically significant positive correlation with PAYEMS growth over any 7-year rolling window, or the correlation is consistently negative.

mechanism_narrative: Unexpectedly high inflation (CPIAUCSL) can lead to immediate concerns about future monetary tightening and erosion of purchasing power. This causes a short-term negative reaction in equity markets over a 5-day horizon as investors price in higher discount rates and potential economic slowdowns.
failure_condition: If, when CPIAUCSL month-over-month growth exceeds 0.5%, the S&P 500 5-day forward returns are positive in more than 55% of instances over a 10-year period.

mechanism_narrative: When GDP growth (GDPC1) is strong but industrial production (INDPRO) is lagging, it suggests a consumption-driven economy without strong underlying productive capacity. This can lead to inflationary pressures and eventually dampen long-term equity returns over a 60-day horizon as imbalances become apparent.
candidate_trigger: GDPC1 year-over-year growth > 3% AND INDPRO year-over-year growth < 1%
failure_condition: If, during periods where GDPC1 growth is strong and INDPRO growth is weak, the S&P 500 60-day forward returns are consistently positive (e.g., average return > 0.5% per month) over a 10-year period.
